## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time — open a doc, edit it live with others, see everyone's cursors, manage who has access — then go deep on the two or three hardest parts, which for Google Docs are real-time conflict resolution and durable versioned storage. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension here: many people typing into the *same* document *at the same time*, and everyone must converge to the *identical* final text in under a heartbeat — without anyone's keystrokes getting lost or reordered. That's the whole problem in one sentence.

> 💬 "The hard part isn't storing a document — it's that two people can edit the same character position in the same 50 milliseconds, and both edits have to survive and land in a consistent order for everyone."

I'll target a **Google-style bar** — go deep on the one distributed-systems problem (concurrent-edit convergence) and show the rejected alternative. Tell me if it's Amazon / Meta / a startup and I'll re-tilt the emphasis.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**F1 — Open / load a document.** A user opens a doc and sees its current content within ~1 second, with their access level (view / comment / edit) enforced.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I click a doc link and the whole thing shows up almost instantly, already up to date with what everyone else has typed."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** we can't replay millions of historical keystrokes on every open — we need a recent *snapshot* plus only the edits since, or loads get slower as a doc ages.

**F2 — Real-time collaborative editing.** Multiple users edit the same document simultaneously; every keystroke propagates to all other editors within a few hundred milliseconds, and everyone converges to the same text.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When my colleague types a word, I see it appear under their cursor a moment later — and even if we both type at the exact same instant, we don't end up with two scrambled versions."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** edits are relative to positions ("insert 'x' at index 12") — but if someone else inserted text before index 12 a millisecond earlier, my index 12 is now wrong. Resolving this is *operational transformation* / *CRDTs*, the heart of the problem.

**F3 — Presence & live cursors.** Each editor sees who else is in the doc and where their cursor / selection is, updating live as people move.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I see little colored cursors with names floating around the page so I know who's looking at what and won't talk over them."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** cursor positions change dozens of times a second per user — this is a high-frequency, *throwaway* stream that must NOT be persisted or it would drown the real edit log.

**F4 — Persistence & version history.** Edits are durably saved continuously, and a user can open version history to see and restore past states.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I never hit save — it's always saved — and I can scroll back through every version and roll back to last Tuesday if I mess up."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** storing every keystroke forever is huge; storing only the latest loses history. We need *delta storage* (save the edits) plus periodic *snapshots*, and a clean way to reconstruct any point in time.

**F5 — Permissions & sharing.** An owner controls who can view / comment / edit, via direct shares or link-based access; every operation checks access.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I decide who gets in and what they can do, and someone with view-only access physically cannot change the text even if they try in the browser console."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** access must be checked on *every* edit op at the server, not just at open — a viewer's tab could try to send edit ops directly, and a revoked user must be kicked mid-session.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** rich-media embeds (images/drawings), comments & suggestions mode, offline editing with later sync, full-text search across all docs, mobile-specific concerns, exports to PDF/Word. I'll mention where each would hook in.

### Non-Functional

**N1 — Sync latency.** Edits propagate editor-to-editor at p99 < 200 ms within a region.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Less than a fifth of a second from your keystroke to my screen — fast enough that it feels like we're at the same keyboard."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** typing feels laggy and "rubber-bandy"; cursors jump; people start typing over each other because they're seeing stale text. Collaboration stops feeling live.

**N2 — Consistency / convergence (strong, per-document).** All editors of a doc MUST converge to byte-identical content. No lost updates, ever.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Everyone in the doc ends up looking at exactly the same words — there's never a moment where my copy and yours have silently drifted apart."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** two people's edits scramble each other — a sentence becomes garbled, a paragraph duplicates or vanishes. This is the single most unforgivable bug in a doc editor.

**N3 — Durability.** An acknowledged edit is never lost. RPO ≈ 0 for committed ops.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Once the doc shows your change as saved, it's truly saved — a server crash one second later can't lose it."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a user writes a paragraph, sees "All changes saved," the server crashes, and the work is gone. Total loss of trust.

**N4 — Availability.** 99.99% on the read/edit path (≈ 52 min/year of downtime).
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Four nines — the service is unreachable less than an hour across an entire year."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** users can't open or edit docs during a meeting or deadline — for a productivity tool, an outage during work hours is catastrophic.

**N5 — Scale.** ~100M daily active users, millions of concurrent live editing sessions, with single hot documents (a viral shared doc) handled gracefully.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Hundreds of millions of people a day, millions editing live at once, and we don't fall over when one document suddenly has thousands of people in it."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the system works in a demo but melts the moment a popular public doc (a petition, a class worksheet) draws a crowd to one document — a classic hot-shard failure.

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** rich formatting (bold, tables, images) makes the operation model much richer than plain-text insert/delete — I'll design the OT/CRDT model for character ops and note that formatting attributes ride along as a parallel op type. And offline editing (long-disconnected clients merging back) is the nastiest convergence case — I'll scope it to short disconnects today and call out the hard version in the ledger.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So Google Docs is really five flows: open a doc fast, edit it live with other people, see their cursors, never lose a change, and control who's allowed in. The whole design lives or dies on two non-functionals — sync has to feel instant, under about 200 milliseconds, and every editor has to converge to byte-identical text with zero lost updates. Everything else — durability, four-nines availability, surviving a hot document — matters, but if I get convergence wrong nothing else saves me. I'm assuming a Google-scale bar: about 100 million daily users and millions editing at once."

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Before I size it — are we good treating this as plain-text-with-formatting-attributes, and deferring full offline editing? That keeps the conflict model tractable while still covering the real-time core."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — How many people can edit one document at the same time?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If it's a handful, I can keep all the merge logic for one doc on a single server, which is simple. If it's thousands in one doc, that one document becomes a hot-spot and I need a totally different fan-out story."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If FEW (≤ ~50) → one doc lives on one "collab server," in-memory model, dead simple. If MANY (thousands) → I need a fan-out tier and possibly to relax that everyone sees every keystroke (batch cursor/edit broadcasts).
- **Assumption:** typical doc has 2–10 concurrent editors, but I'll design so a hot doc with thousands of viewers degrades gracefully (viewers get batched updates, editors stay real-time).

**Q2 — Do we need to support offline editing and later merge?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Offline is the single hardest case — a laptop edits for two hours disconnected, then rejoins and has to merge cleanly. It changes my conflict-resolution choice entirely."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If YES → CRDTs become very attractive because they merge any two divergent histories without a central referee. If NO (short blips only) → server-authoritative OT is simpler and is what I'll lead with.
- **Assumption:** short disconnects (seconds, a flaky wifi moment) are in scope; multi-hour offline is out of scope today, flagged in the ledger.

**Q3 — What's the consistency expectation — can users briefly see different text?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If a half-second of divergence is fine as long as it converges, I have lots of room. If not, I'm forced to serialize every edit through one authority per doc."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If eventual-but-converging is OK → I can broadcast optimistically and reconcile. If must-never-diverge-visibly → stricter single-writer-per-doc ordering.
- **Assumption:** brief optimistic divergence (you see your own keystroke instantly, others' arrive ~100 ms later) is fine, as long as we *always* converge. This is how real Docs feels.

**Q4 — How much version history do we keep, and at what granularity?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Keeping every keystroke forever is a storage bomb; keeping daily snapshots is cheap but loses fine-grained undo. The answer sets my storage budget."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If fine-grained forever → keep the full op log, archive cold. If named/auto versions only → snapshot periodically, compact ops between.
- **Assumption:** keep the full op log for recent activity (e.g. 30 days) for fine undo, plus periodic named snapshots forever; compact old ops into snapshots.

**Q5 — Rich text and formatting, or plain text?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Formatting (bold, headings, tables, images) turns my simple insert/delete op model into a much richer one. I want to know how deep to go."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If rich → ops carry attributes and there are attribute-only ops; the OT transform functions get more cases. If plain → just insert/delete on a character sequence.
- **Assumption:** rich text, but I'll model the *core* on character insert/delete and treat formatting as attribute ops riding the same pipeline.

**Q6 — Single region or global users in the same doc?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Two editors on opposite sides of the planet in the same doc means cross-region latency I can't beat with physics — it changes whether one server can be the authority."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If single-region per doc → one home region owns the doc, others relay (simpler, what I'll do). If true multi-master global → much harder convergence and conflict windows.
- **Assumption:** each document has a single "home region" that owns its authoritative ordering; far users connect through to it. Cross-region collaboration works but with that region's latency.

**Q7 — How are permissions modeled — per-user, per-link, org-wide?**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "It decides whether an authorization check is a fast cache hit or a heavy graph walk through org/group membership."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If simple per-user/per-link → a small ACL row per doc. If org + nested groups → I need a permissions service with group expansion and caching.
- **Assumption:** per-user grants + link-based access (anyone-with-link: view/comment/edit), with a permissions service and a short-TTL cache; nested org groups noted as an extension.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "My headline assumptions: server-authoritative OT, single home region per doc, rich text modeled on character ops, full op log for 30 days plus permanent snapshots, and a few-to-dozens editors typical with hot docs degrading gracefully. Happy for me to size it on those?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll compute only the few numbers that actually drive the design, and defer the rest. *(QPS — queries per second, how many requests hit a server each second. p99 — the latency 99% of requests beat.)*

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DAU | given | 100M | "a hundred million daily" | sets the base |
| Concurrent edit sessions (avg) | 100M DAU × 2 sessions/day × (15 min ÷ 1440 min/day) = 100M × 2 × 0.0104 ≈ 2.08M | ~2M | "about 2 million people editing at any moment" | WebSocket fleet size |
| Peak concurrent (≈3× avg) | 2.08M × 3 ≈ 6.25M | ~6M | "about 6 million live connections at peak" | **the binding number** |
| WebSocket servers | 6.25M ÷ 50K conns/server | ~125 | "around 125 connection servers" | fleet/cost |
| Active typers at peak | 6.25M × 20% typing at once = 1.25M | ~1.25M | "a bit over a million actually typing" | edit-ingest sizing |
| Edit ops/sec (peak) | 1.25M typers × 5 ops/s (batched keystrokes) ≈ 6.25M | ~6M ops/s | "about 6 million edits a second" | the write hot-path |
| Broadcast msgs/sec | 6.25M ops × ~2 (avg collaborators to fan out to) ≈ 12.5M | ~12M/s | "roughly 12 million broadcast messages a second" | fan-out tier |
| Doc-open QPS (peak) | 100M × 2 opens ÷ 86,400s × 3 ≈ 6,900 | ~7K/s | "about 7 thousand doc loads a second" | snapshot read tier |
| Snapshot storage | 50B docs × 30 KB avg = 1.5 PB; ×3 replication ≈ 4.6 PB | ~5 PB | "a few petabytes" | non-issue, blob store |
| Op log | 6M avg-ish ops/s × 100 B × 86,400 ≈ 18 TB/day → ~6.5 PB/yr before compaction | ~18 TB/day | "tens of terabytes a day raw" | why we compact to snapshots |

*Term glosses:* **WebSocket** *(a persistent two-way connection the browser holds open so the server can push edits down instantly without the client polling — here it carries every keystroke both ways).* **Op / operation** *(one atomic edit, like "insert 'h' at position 12" — the unit we transform and store).* **Snapshot** *(the full document content saved at a point in time, so we don't replay millions of ops to load it).*

**The ONE number that forces the design: ~6 million concurrent live connections and ~6 million edit ops/sec at peak.** But the real subtlety is *distribution* — that's spread over millions of docs, so per-doc it's tiny. The binding ceiling isn't aggregate throughput; it's the **single hot document**: a public doc with thousands of editors all serialized through one authoritative ordering. A single collab server processing one doc's ordered ops tops out around ~10K ops/s for that one doc — that's the flip threshold where I must batch viewer updates and cap real-time editors.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number I care about is roughly 6 million live connections at peak, and about the same in edits per second. But that's spread across millions of documents, so per document it's nothing — which tells me the system isn't bottlenecked in aggregate. The thing that actually bites is one hot document with thousands of people in it, because all its edits have to be ordered by a single authority. So I'll spend my design budget on per-document ordering and on degrading a hot doc gracefully — not on raw aggregate throughput, which sharding handles for free."

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the nouns; I'll add full field definitions in §7 (Data Model).

**Document** — the logical doc: its metadata (title, owner, home region) and a pointer to its current content snapshot + op log. It OWNS identity and the authoritative ordering of edits.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of it like a shared whiteboard with a serial number — the board itself plus a logbook of every mark made on it, in order."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How do you load it fast if it has a million edits?" → answer: we never replay all ops; we load the latest *snapshot* and apply only the handful of ops since it.

**Operation (Op)** — one atomic edit: insert/delete/format at a position, stamped with a revision number and author. It OWNS the change history and is the unit of conflict resolution.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a single tracked-changes entry — 'Anshul inserted "x" after the 12th character' — that we can replay, transform, or undo."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Two ops target the same position at once — what happens?" → answer: the server transforms the second op against the first (OT) so both apply meaningfully and everyone converges.

**Snapshot / Revision** — the materialized full content at a specific revision number, written periodically so loads and history are fast.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a 'save point' in a game — the full state frozen, so you don't replay the whole level to get back there."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How often do you snapshot?" → answer: every N ops or T seconds, whichever first — balances load speed against write cost.

**Session / Presence** — a user's live connection to a doc: their cursor, selection, color, and online status. Ephemeral — it OWNS "who's here right now," nothing persisted.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like the little colored dots on a map showing where each person is standing — gone the instant they leave, never written in a logbook."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Doesn't cursor spam overwhelm storage?" → answer: presence is never persisted — it's a fire-and-forget pub/sub stream with a heartbeat TTL.

**Permission / ACL entry** — who can do what on a doc (owner/editor/commenter/viewer), per-user or per-link. It OWNS access decisions.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a guest list with role stamps at the door — and the bouncer checks it on *every* action, not just entry."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How do you revoke access mid-edit?" → answer: the collab server holds the permission with a short TTL and re-checks; on revoke we push a disconnect and reject further ops.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The nouns are Document, Operation, Snapshot, Presence, and Permission. The one people underweight is the Operation — it's not just a log entry, it's the unit of conflict resolution, and getting its position semantics right is the whole game. Presence is the opposite — it looks like data but it's deliberately throwaway, never persisted. I'll flesh out fields, keys, and stores in the data model section."

---

## 5. API / Interface

Default is REST for the control plane (open, share, history) and a **WebSocket** channel for the live edit stream — because edits must be *pushed* server-to-client with no polling. Security: the server identifies the caller from their session cookie / JWT *(JSON Web Token — a signed token proving who you are, so the server never trusts a user id sent in the request body)*, and re-checks permission on every edit op.

**1. Open a document — `GET /v1/docs/{docId}`** — GET because it only reads; it returns the latest snapshot + revision number + a WebSocket URL to connect for live edits.
- Key request fields: `docId: string` (path) — which doc. No body. The caller's identity comes from the auth token, **not** a `userId` field — "the client never tells me who it is; I read that from the signed token, so a viewer can't impersonate the owner."
- Key response fields: `snapshot` (the content), `revision: int` (the version the snapshot is at — the client applies later ops on top), `wsUrl` + `wsToken` (a short-lived token scoped to this doc + permission), `role` (so the UI greys out editing for viewers).
```
// Request
GET /v1/docs/doc_8fk2 

// Response 200
{
  "docId": "doc_8fk2",
  "revision": 10432,
  "snapshot": { "type": "rich-text", "content": "..." },
  "role": "editor",
  "wsUrl": "wss://collab-7.docs.example.com/doc_8fk2",
  "wsToken": "wst_a1b2..."
}
```
- **Key design decision:** load = snapshot + revision, never the full op log. The client opens the WebSocket, says "I'm at revision 10432," and the server streams only ops after that. Loads stay O(1) regardless of doc age.

**2. Live edit channel — `WSS /doc/{docId}` (message: `submitOps`)** — a persistent socket, not REST, because edits flow both directions continuously and must be pushed with no polling latency.
- Key client→server message fields: `baseRevision: int` (the revision this op was authored against — the server uses it to know what to transform against), `ops: [...]` (the edits), `clientOpId: string` (idempotency key — a per-client unique id so a retried op after a network blip isn't applied twice).
- Server→client messages: `ack {revision}` (your op committed at this new revision) and `broadcast {ops, revision, authorId}` (someone else's transformed ops to apply).
```
// Client -> server
{ "type": "submitOps", "baseRevision": 10432,
  "clientOpId": "c17:42", "ops": [ {"insert": "hello", "at": 88} ] }

// Server -> client (to the author)
{ "type": "ack", "revision": 10433 }

// Server -> all other editors
{ "type": "broadcast", "revision": 10433, "authorId": "u_55",
  "ops": [ {"insert": "hello", "at": 88} ] }
```
- **Key design decision:** the op carries `baseRevision`, not absolute trust in `at`. If the server has advanced past `baseRevision`, it **transforms** the incoming op against the intervening ops before committing — this is OT, and it's why no edit is lost. `clientOpId` makes the submit idempotent across reconnects.

**3. Presence / cursor — `WSS` (message: `cursor`)** — same socket, separate fire-and-forget message; never persisted.
- Fields: `position: int`, `selectionEnd: int`, `color`/`name` (cached). No ack, no durability — "if a cursor update drops, who cares, the next one's 50 ms away."
- **Key design decision:** presence rides a *separate, lossy* pub/sub path so it can never back-pressure or pollute the durable edit log.

**4. Version history — `GET /v1/docs/{docId}/revisions`** — GET, read-only; returns the list of named/auto snapshots.
- Fields: `from`, `to` (revision or time range). Returns snapshot metadata, not full content (fetch a specific one separately). 
- **Key design decision:** history is served from stored snapshots + the op log between them, reconstructed on demand — we don't keep every intermediate full document.

**5. Restore a version — `POST /v1/docs/{docId}/restore`** — POST because it *creates new state* (a restore is itself a new revision, not a destructive rewind, so history is preserved).
- Fields: `targetRevision: int`, `clientOpId` (idempotency). Server computes the diff from current → target and applies it as a fresh op so collaborators see it live.
- **Key design decision:** restore is an *additive* op, never a delete of history — "you can undo the undo."

**6. Share / change permissions — `PUT /v1/docs/{docId}/permissions`** — PUT replaces the ACL for a principal; only the owner/editor-with-share-rights may call it.
- Fields: `principal` (userId or `"link"`), `role` (`viewer|commenter|editor`). Server verifies the *caller* may grant before applying. Revocation pushes a disconnect to any live session of the revoked user.
- **Key design decision:** permission changes propagate to live collab servers immediately (cache invalidation + push), so a revoked viewer is kicked within seconds, not at next open.

> 💬 "Notice the client never sends who it is or what role it has — that's all derived from the signed token server-side. The most an attacker's browser can do is send edit ops, and the collab server rejects them if the token's scope is view-only."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "There are really two interfaces. A small REST surface for the lifecycle — open, share, history, restore — and one long-lived WebSocket per open doc that carries the live edits and cursors. The clever bit is the open call returns a snapshot plus a revision number, and the socket streams only the ops since that revision, so opening a giant old doc is just as fast as a new one. Every edit op carries the revision it was based on and a client-side op id — the first lets the server transform it for convergence, the second makes retries safe."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Color palette I'll reuse in every diagram: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = async/queues, grey = datastores, purple = external.

### 6.1 "Open / load a document" (F1)

A user opens a doc; we must return up-to-date content in ~1 second and hand them a live channel — without replaying history.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| API Gateway | The front door that authenticates the request and routes it | Need one place to verify the JWT and rate-limit before anything hits our services | Not per-service auth (duplicated, error-prone); not no-gateway (no central throttle) | One more hop (~5 ms) |
| Document Service | Stateless service that fetches the snapshot + revision and issues a `wsToken` | Keeps load logic in one place and decouples it from the stateful collab tier | Not folding this into the collab server (would couple stateless reads to stateful sessions) | Extra service to run |
| Snapshot Store (blob) | Object storage (like S3/GCS) holding the materialized document content | Snapshots are large, immutable blobs read whole — exactly what object storage is cheapest and most durable for | Not Postgres BLOBs (expensive, bloats the DB); not local disk (not durable/shared) | Slightly higher per-read latency than a DB row (fine, it's cached) |
| Metadata DB | Relational DB row per doc: title, owner, home region, current revision, snapshot pointer | Need a consistent place for "what revision is this doc at + where's its snapshot" with transactions | Not a KV store (we want transactional updates of revision + pointer together) | Must shard by docId at scale |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "When you open a doc, the request hits the API gateway, which checks your token and forwards to the Document Service. That service looks up the doc's metadata row — owner, current revision, and a pointer to its latest snapshot — then pulls that snapshot from object storage. Object storage because a snapshot is a big immutable blob and that's the cheapest, most durable place for it; I'd never stuff it in Postgres. It returns the content plus the revision number and a short-lived WebSocket token scoped to this doc and your role. The trade-off is an extra service hop, but it keeps the stateless read path cleanly separated from the stateful editing tier."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **API Gateway** — the single entry point that authenticates and routes every request. Here it verifies your token and rate-limits before any work happens.
> **Snapshot Store / object storage** — a service that stores large files (blobs) cheaply and durably, addressed by key. Here it holds each doc's materialized content so we never rebuild it from scratch.
> **Revision number** — a monotonically increasing integer version stamped on the doc after each committed op. Here it lets the client say "I have up to 10432, send me what's newer."

**Diagram — Open flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Browser / Docs client]
    Client -->|"1: GET /docs/id"| GW[API Gateway]
    subgraph ReadPath["Open / load path"]
        GW -->|"2: route"| DS[Document Service]
        DS -->|"3: read meta + revision"| META[(Metadata DB)]
        DS -->|"4: fetch snapshot"| BLOB[(Snapshot Store)]
    end
    DS -->|"5: snapshot + revision + wsToken"| Client
    Client -.->|"6: connect with revision"| COLLAB[Collab Server]

    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class GW,DS,COLLAB svc
    class META,BLOB db
```

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Client → Gateway (`GET /docs/id`).** The browser requests the doc. The gateway owns auth, so it verifies the token here; if invalid we stop before touching any service. *What if the token's expired?* → 401, client refreshes and retries.
2. **Gateway → Document Service.** Routed to the stateless read service because load logic is its job, separate from live editing. State change: none yet.
3. **Document Service → Metadata DB.** Reads the doc row to get current revision + snapshot pointer + the caller's role. The DB owns the authoritative "current revision." *What if the row's missing?* → 404.
4. **Document Service → Snapshot Store.** Fetches the immutable snapshot blob (usually cache-hit in a CDN/edge cache). *What if the blob read fails?* → fall back to reconstructing from the previous snapshot + op log; slower but correct.
5. **Service → Client.** Returns content + revision + a scoped `wsToken`. Now the client can render and is told exactly which revision it's holding.
6. **Client → Collab Server (WebSocket).** The client opens the live channel announcing "I'm at revision 10432"; the collab server streams any ops since then. State change: a live session now exists.

> 💬 "The key move is load equals snapshot plus revision number — I hand you a save-point and a version tag, and the socket fills in only what's changed since. Opening a five-year-old doc is as fast as a new one."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Opening a doc is a stateless read: gateway authenticates, Document Service grabs the metadata row and the latest snapshot from object storage, and returns the content with a revision number and a socket token. The snapshot lives in blob storage because it's a big immutable file — Postgres would be the wrong tool. The whole trick is that I return a revision number too, so when the client connects its live socket it only needs the handful of ops since that revision. That keeps load time flat no matter how old the document is."

### 6.2 "Real-time collaborative editing" (F2) — the heart

Multiple editors type into the same doc; every op must reach everyone and all copies must converge. This is where the design is won or lost.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Collab Server (stateful) | A server that "owns" a set of live docs in memory, orders their ops, and broadcasts | One authority per doc gives a single serialization point → clean convergence | Not stateless app servers (no single ordering point → divergence); not client-only P2P (no referee) | Stateful → needs sticky routing + failover |
| OT Engine | The transform logic: rewrites an incoming op against ops it didn't see, so it still applies correctly | Server-authoritative OT is proven for text and cheap on memory vs CRDTs | Not CRDT (more memory/metadata per char; we don't need offline merge today); not last-writer-wins (loses edits) | OT transform functions are tricky to get exactly right |
| Routing layer | Maps each docId to its current owning collab server (consistent hashing + registry) | Need every editor of a doc to land on the *same* server | Not random LB (editors split across servers → no shared order) | A registry to keep current on failover |
| Op Log (append-only) | Durable ordered log of committed ops per doc | Must persist each op before ack for durability (RPO≈0) and for history/replay | Not "snapshot only" (loses fine history + risks loss between snapshots) | Storage grows; compact into snapshots |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The core decision is that each document has exactly one collab server that owns it — that single server is the referee that decides the order of edits. When your op arrives, it carries the revision you based it on. If the server has already committed other ops past that revision, it transforms your op against them — that's operational transformation, OT — so your insert still lands where you meant relative to what's now there, and nobody's edit is lost. I considered CRDTs, which let any two replicas merge without a referee — great for offline — but they carry more metadata per character and I don't need offline merge today, so OT is the leaner choice. Last-writer-wins I reject outright because it silently drops edits. The op is appended to a durable log before I ack, so a crash can't lose an acknowledged change. The cost is that the collab server is stateful, so I need sticky routing and a failover story — which I'll cover in the deep dive."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Operational Transformation (OT)** — an algorithm that rewrites one edit against concurrent edits it didn't account for, so all sites applying ops in any order still end up identical. Here it's how two simultaneous inserts both survive and converge.
> **CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type)** — a data structure where any two replicas that have seen the same set of edits are guaranteed identical, with no central coordinator. Here it's the alternative we'd pick if offline editing were in scope.
> **Sticky routing** — sending all of a doc's editors to the same server instance. Here it guarantees a single ordering authority per doc.
> **Append-only Op Log** — a durable log we only ever add to, never modify. Here it makes every committed edit recoverable and replayable.

**Diagram — Edit / sync flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EdA[Editor A]
    EdB[Editor B]
    EdA -->|"1: submitOps base=R"| COLLAB[Collab Server]
    EdB -.->|"1b: submitOps base=R"| COLLAB
    subgraph EditPath["Edit ordering + sync"]
        COLLAB -->|"2: transform vs newer ops"| OT[OT Engine]
        OT -->|"3: ordered op @ R+1"| COLLAB
        COLLAB -->|"4: append before ack"| OPLOG[(Op Log)]
    end
    COLLAB -->|"5: ack R+1 to author"| EdA
    COLLAB -->|"6: broadcast op @ R+1"| EdB
    COLLAB -.->|"7: every N ops"| SNAP[Snapshot Worker]
    SNAP -.->|"8: write snapshot"| BLOB[(Snapshot Store)]

    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class EdA,EdB client
    class COLLAB,OT svc
    class SNAP queue
    class OPLOG,BLOB db
```
↳ reuses existing: Snapshot Store (from §6.1)

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Editors A & B → Collab Server (`submitOps`, base=R).** Both submit ops based on revision R. They land on the *same* collab server because routing is sticky by docId — that's what makes a single order possible. *What if they hit different servers?* → there'd be no shared order; sticky routing + the registry prevent this.
2. **Collab Server → OT Engine.** A's op commits first at R+1. B's op was also based on R, but R is now stale, so the server hands B's op to the OT engine to transform against A's. *What if transform is wrong?* → divergence — this is the riskiest code, covered + tested in the deep dive.
3. **OT Engine → Collab Server.** Returns B's op rewritten so it still means what B intended relative to A's change. State: B's op is now safe to commit at R+2.
4. **Collab Server → Op Log (append before ack).** The committed op is durably appended *before* we acknowledge. State change: the edit is now permanent. *What if the log write fails?* → we DON'T ack; client retries with the same `clientOpId` (idempotent).
5. **Collab Server → Author (ack R+1).** The author learns their op committed and at what revision, so its local op id reconciles.
6. **Collab Server → other editors (broadcast).** The transformed op streams to everyone else, who apply it at the new revision. State: all copies converge.
7–8. **Collab Server → Snapshot Worker → Snapshot Store (async).** Every N ops the worker materializes a fresh snapshot so loads and history stay fast — off the hot path.

> 💬 "One server per doc is the referee. Every edit gets a revision number from it, and any edit based on a stale revision gets transformed before it commits — that's how two people typing in the same spot both win and everyone converges."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "All editors of a doc are routed to one collab server, which is the single authority for ordering. When two ops collide — both based on the same revision — the first commits, and the second is transformed against it with OT so it still lands correctly; nothing is lost. Every committed op is appended to a durable log *before* we ack, so an acknowledged edit survives a crash. We broadcast the transformed op to everyone else and they converge. Asynchronously, a worker snapshots every N ops so the document never has to be rebuilt from its full history. The one thing I have to nail is the transform functions — they're subtle, so they're unit-tested exhaustively."

### 6.3 "Presence & live cursors" (F3)

Show who's in the doc and where their cursor is, updating many times per second — without polluting the durable edit path.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Presence channel | A lossy pub/sub stream on the same socket for cursor/selection | Cursor data is high-frequency and disposable — it must never touch the op log | Not persisting cursors (storage + write amplification for zero value) | Cursors can briefly lag/jump — fine |
| Presence store (in-mem) | A short-TTL in-memory map (e.g. Redis) of "who's online in doc X" | Need fast "who's here" with auto-expiry on disconnect via heartbeat TTL | Not a durable DB (it's ephemeral; TTL expiry is the whole point) | State lost on Redis restart — rebuilt from live heartbeats in seconds |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Presence rides the same WebSocket but on a totally separate, lossy path. Cursor positions change dozens of times a second and have zero long-term value, so they go to an in-memory store with a short time-to-live — if a client stops heartbeating, it expires and drops off the 'who's online' list automatically. The deliberate choice is that this path can drop messages freely; the next cursor update is 50 milliseconds away. The point is to keep this firehose completely away from the durable op log so it can never back-pressure real edits."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Pub/sub** — a messaging pattern where publishers send to a channel and all subscribers receive it. Here it fans cursor updates to everyone in the doc.
> **TTL (time-to-live)** — an automatic expiry on a stored value. Here a presence entry expires if heartbeats stop, so disconnected users vanish without explicit cleanup.

↳ builds on §6.2 diagram — no new persistent components; presence is a separate lossy channel on the existing socket + an in-memory Redis map.

**Numbered narration:** (1) client sends `cursor {pos}` on the socket several times/sec; (2) collab server writes it to the in-memory presence map with a few-second TTL and (3) fans it out to other editors via pub/sub; (4) on disconnect the heartbeat stops and the TTL expires the entry, so others see the cursor disappear. *What if presence is wrong/stale?* → cosmetically a ghost cursor lingers a second or two; never affects document correctness.

> 💬 "Presence is fire-and-forget on purpose — I'd rather drop a cursor frame than ever let cursor spam slow down a real edit."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Presence is the easy twin of editing — same socket, opposite guarantees. Cursors go to an in-memory store with a short TTL and fan out via pub/sub; if a client goes quiet, its entry just expires and everyone sees it leave. I never persist any of it, because the value of a cursor position three seconds ago is zero, and keeping it separate protects the durable edit log from a high-frequency firehose."

### 6.4 "Persistence & version history" (F4)

Edits are already durably logged (§6.2); here we make history viewable and restorable efficiently using **snapshot + diff**.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Snapshot Worker | Async job that materializes a full snapshot every N ops / T seconds | Bounds replay cost: any revision = nearest snapshot + a few ops | Not snapshot-every-op (huge storage); not never-snapshot (slow loads) | Snapshots use storage; tunable cadence |
| History Service | Reconstructs any past revision from snapshot + op log on demand | History is read-rarely; compute on demand beats storing every version | Not storing every full version (storage explosion) | A history read does a little replay work |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Version history is the snapshot-plus-diff strategy. Because every op is in the durable log and we snapshot periodically, reconstructing any past point is just 'grab the nearest snapshot and replay the ops up to the revision you want.' Restore is interesting: I make it *additive* — I compute the diff from now back to the target and apply it as a brand-new op, so collaborators see it live and you can even undo the restore. I never destroy history."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Snapshot + diff** — store occasional full snapshots plus the small edits between them; reconstruct any version by taking the nearest snapshot and applying diffs. Here it makes history cheap to store and fast to read.

↳ builds on §6.2 diagram — adds the History Service reading the existing Op Log + Snapshot Store.

**Numbered narration:** (1) `GET /revisions` → History Service lists snapshots from metadata; (2) to view revision R it loads the nearest snapshot ≤ R from blob and (3) replays op-log entries up to R; (4) `POST /restore` computes current→target diff and (5) submits it as a normal op through the collab server so it's live + reversible. *What if the op log has a gap?* → snapshots are the recovery floor; we can always fall back to the last good snapshot.

> 💬 "History is free because I already store every op and snapshot periodically — any version is just nearest-snapshot-plus-replay, and restore is a new edit, not a rewind."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "I don't store every version of the document — I store periodic snapshots plus the op log between them, and reconstruct any point on demand by replaying from the nearest snapshot. Restore is deliberately additive: it's a new op that transforms the current text into the old text, so it shows up live for everyone and history stays intact. The snapshot cadence is the one knob — more frequent means faster history reads but more storage."

### 6.5 "Permissions & sharing" (F5)

Every op and open must check access; revocation must take effect mid-session.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Permissions Service | Owns ACL rows (principal → role) and answers "can user U do action A on doc D?" | One authority for access keeps checks consistent and auditable | Not embedding ACLs in each service (drift, no single audit point) | One more dependency on the hot path → cache it |
| ACL cache | Short-TTL cache of permission decisions at the collab server | Avoid a permission lookup on every keystroke | Not no-cache (a lookup per op would crush the service) | A revoked user may linger up to the TTL → push invalidation to fix |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "There's a dedicated Permissions Service that owns the access-control list and answers 'can this user do this here.' The collab server can't call it on every keystroke, so it caches the decision with a short TTL. The subtlety is revocation: when an owner removes someone, I don't wait for the TTL — I push an invalidation to the collab server, which drops the cache entry and disconnects that user's live session, rejecting any further ops. So a revoked viewer is out within seconds. The trade-off is a small window where a just-revoked user could still send an op, bounded by how fast the push propagates."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **ACL (Access Control List)** — the list of who has what role on a resource. Here it's the per-doc guest list the system checks on every action.
> **Cache invalidation (push)** — proactively telling caches to drop a stale entry. Here it makes permission revocation near-instant instead of waiting for TTL expiry.

    COLLAB -->|"5: check ACL cache"| COLLAB

    COLLAB -->|"7: reject if revoked / disconnect"| Editor

**Numbered narration:** (1) owner calls `PUT /permissions`; (2) Permissions Service writes the ACL row (after checking the owner *may* grant); (3) it pushes an invalidation to any collab server hosting the doc; (4) when an editor submits ops, (5) the collab server checks its short-TTL ACL cache, (6) on miss it looks up the Permissions Service, and (7) if the user was revoked it rejects the op and disconnects them. *What if the push is lost?* → the TTL is the backstop; worst case the user lingers until the cache entry expires (seconds).

> 💬 "Permissions are checked on every op, not just at open — and revocation pushes a kick, so a view-only tab trying to inject edits gets bounced server-side."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Access control is its own service owning the ACLs, but the collab server caches decisions so it isn't hit on every keystroke. The interesting case is revocation: I push an invalidation that drops the cache and disconnects the user's live session immediately, with the short TTL as a backstop if the push is missed. So sharing changes feel instant, and a viewer physically can't sneak edits past the server."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Document | **docId (PK, shard key)**, title, ownerId, homeRegion, currentRevision, snapshotPtr | Metadata DB (sharded SQL) | docId | Strong (revision + pointer updated atomically) |
| Operation | **(docId, revision) (PK)**, authorId, opType, payload, clientOpId, ts | Op Log (append-only, e.g. Bigtable/Cassandra/Kafka-backed) | docId | Strong order *within* a doc |
| Snapshot | **(docId, revision) (PK)**, contentBlobKey, sizeBytes, createdAt | Snapshot Store (object storage) + index row in Metadata DB | docId | Immutable once written |
| Permission | **(docId, principalId) (PK)**, role, grantedBy, ts | Permissions DB (SQL) | docId | Strong |
| Presence | (docId, userId) → cursorPos, color, lastSeen | Redis (in-memory, TTL) | docId | Ephemeral / best-effort |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ Metadata DB (sharded relational, e.g. Spanner / Postgres) — used for: Document, Snapshot index**
1. **What it is:** a relational database storing data in rows with strict schemas and transactions — a multi-step update either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** we must update a doc's `currentRevision` *and* its `snapshotPtr` together, atomically, and read them consistently — if those two ever disagree, a load returns a snapshot at the wrong revision. Only a transactional store guarantees that pairing.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **Plain KV store (Dynamo/Redis)** — fast, but no multi-field transaction; revision + pointer could tear apart under concurrent writes.
   - **A single global SQL instance** — simplest, but can't hold 50B doc rows or the write rate; we must shard by docId.
   - **MongoDB** — workable, but we want strict transactional semantics on the revision pointer and standard SQL tooling.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** sharding by docId means cross-doc queries (e.g. "all docs in a folder") need a secondary index/service; each shard is independent.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Doc metadata goes in a sharded relational store because I need to bump the revision and the snapshot pointer in one atomic step — if those drift apart I'd serve the wrong content. I shard by docId so it scales horizontally, and I accept that cross-document queries need a separate index."

**🗄️ Op Log (append-only, e.g. Bigtable / Cassandra, or Kafka per-doc partition) — used for: Operation**
1. **What it is:** a store optimized for appending ordered records and reading them back in order, keyed by docId then revision.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** the dominant operation is "append the next op for this doc" at very high aggregate rate (~6M/s) and "read ops from revision X onward." A wide-column/log store nails high-volume ordered appends and range reads; we never update an op in place.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **Relational DB** — fine for metadata, but row-per-op at 6M writes/s with an index would be far more expensive than an append-optimized store.
   - **Pure Kafka** — excellent ordered log, but unbounded random-access reads of old ranges (for history) are awkward; we'd still need a queryable store. (We may use Kafka as the *ingest* buffer in front.)
4. **Trade-off we accept:** the log grows fast (~18 TB/day raw); we compact old ops into snapshots and tier cold data to cheaper storage.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Ops go in an append-only, ordered store keyed by doc then revision, because the workload is 'append the next edit' and 'read everything since revision X' — that's exactly what a log/wide-column store is built for. A relational table would choke at six million writes a second. The cost is volume, so I compact old ops into snapshots."

**🗄️ Snapshot Store (object storage, S3/GCS) — used for: Snapshot content**
1. **What it is:** a service that stores large immutable files (blobs) cheaply and very durably (11 nines of durability), addressed by key.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** snapshots are large (tens of KB to MBs), immutable, and read whole on doc-open — the textbook blob workload, and the cheapest durable home for petabytes.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **BLOBs in the relational DB** — bloats the DB, kills its cache, expensive per GB.
   - **A distributed file system we run** — reinvents what S3/GCS already do better and cheaper.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** slightly higher per-read latency than a DB row — hidden by an edge/CDN cache for hot docs.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Snapshots are big immutable blobs read whole on open, so they belong in object storage — cheapest and most durable place for petabytes. I'd never put them in the database. I front them with a cache so hot docs open instantly."

**🗄️ Redis (in-memory) — used for: Presence, ACL cache**
1. **What it is:** an in-memory key-value store with millisecond access and built-in TTL expiry.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** presence is high-frequency, ephemeral, and must auto-expire on disconnect; a TTL on an in-memory entry is exactly that. Same fit for short-lived permission caching.
3. **What we considered instead:** a durable DB (pointless for throwaway data and far slower); keeping presence only in collab-server memory (fine, but Redis lets multiple servers share presence for a doc spread across instances).
4. **Trade-off we accept:** data lost on Redis restart — acceptable, it rebuilds from live heartbeats within seconds.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Presence and short permission caches live in Redis because they're hot, tiny, and disposable, and Redis gives me automatic expiry. If it restarts I lose nothing that matters — clients re-announce within seconds."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Commit edit op | Op Log + Metadata revision | Strong, ordered per doc | Lost/reordered edits = corruption; must serialize per doc |
| Read-your-own-write (your keystroke) | Collab Server memory | Strong (local echo) | You see your own edit instantly; server reconciles via revision |
| Open doc (snapshot) | Snapshot Store | Read-latest-committed | Must reflect all acked ops up to current revision |
| Presence / cursor | Redis | Best-effort / eventual | A stale cursor is cosmetic |
| Permission check on op | ACL cache → Permissions DB | Strong on grant, bounded-stale on cache | Revocation pushed; TTL backstop bounds the window |

**Read-your-own-writes edge case:** the author always sees their own op immediately (optimistic local apply) and the server's `ack` carries the committed revision; if the socket reconnects, the client replays unacked ops by `clientOpId` so it never double-applies or loses its own edit.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The spine is a sharded relational store for doc metadata — I need revision and snapshot pointer updated atomically — plus an append-only log for the ops themselves, because the workload is high-volume ordered appends that a relational table couldn't take at six million a second. Big immutable snapshots go to object storage, the cheapest durable home, fronted by a cache. Presence and permission caches sit in Redis for speed and auto-expiry. I considered putting ops in a relational DB and rejected it on write cost, and considered pure Kafka but kept a queryable log store so history range-reads stay easy."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the one invariant — *every editor must converge to identical text with zero lost updates* — and reason forward from "what orders the edits?"

### How do we resolve concurrent edits so everyone converges with no lost updates?

#### Bad: Last-writer-wins on whole document
**🗣️ What this is in plain words:** "Whenever someone saves, their whole version overwrites whatever was there."
**Approach:** each client sends the full document on change; the server stores the latest.
**Why people try this:** it's trivial — just a `PUT` of the content.
**⚠️ What breaks:** if A and B both load version 10 and both save, the second save *erases the first person's entire set of changes*. With even 2 active editors you lose work constantly — this violates N2 (convergence) outright.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** any real concurrency at all — two people typing loses data immediately.

#### Good: Server-authoritative Operational Transformation (OT)
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** last-writer-wins overwrote concurrent edits wholesale; OT instead ships *granular ops* and *transforms* concurrent ones so both survive.
**🗣️ What this is in plain words:** "Instead of sending the whole doc, everyone sends tiny edits like 'insert x at position 12,' and a referee rewrites edits that collided so they still make sense and everyone ends up identical."
**Approach:** one collab server is the authority for a doc. Each op carries the `baseRevision` it was made against. When an op arrives based on a stale revision, the server runs **OT** *(an algorithm that rewrites op B against op A so applying A-then-B′ equals applying them as if both authors had seen each other)* against the intervening committed ops, commits the transformed op at the next revision, appends it to the durable log, and broadcasts it. Clients apply incoming ops and reconcile their own via the `ack`'s revision.
**Why this seemed reasonable:** it's the proven approach Google Docs/Wave actually used; memory-light and great for online editing.
**⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** (a) the transform functions are notoriously subtle — every op-pair (insert/insert, insert/delete, delete/delete, with positions) needs a correct transform or you get silent divergence; (b) it *requires* a central authority per doc, so a single hot doc serializes through one server — at ~10K ops/s for one doc that server saturates; (c) it doesn't handle long-offline merges well (a client gone for hours has a huge transform backlog).
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** offline/partition-tolerant editing, or wanting to drop the single-authority requirement, pushes toward CRDTs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Editor A op@R] --> C[Collab Server]
    B[Editor B op@R] --> C
    C -->|"commit A at R+1"| C
    C -->|"transform B vs A"| T[OT transform]
    T -->|"B' at R+2"| C
    C -->|"broadcast A, B'"| Both[All editors converge]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    class A,B,Both client
    class C,T svc
```

#### Great: OT for online + CRDT option for offline; hot-doc graceful degradation
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** plain OT needs a single authority and struggles with long-offline clients and one viral document.
**🗣️ What this is in plain words:** "Keep the fast referee for normal online editing, but for the rare cases — someone offline for hours, or thousands piling into one doc — use a merge structure that needs no referee and stop sending every single keystroke to passive viewers."
**Approach:** (1) Online path stays OT (lean, low-latency). (2) For offline-capable clients, represent the doc as a **CRDT** *(a structure where each character gets a unique, ordered id so any two divergent copies merge to the same result with no central referee — at the cost of per-character metadata)*; a long-offline client merges back by id without a transform backlog. (3) Hot-document handling: cap *real-time editors* per doc (e.g. 50–100); everyone beyond that is a **viewer** receiving *batched* op broadcasts every ~250 ms instead of per-keystroke, collapsing the 12M/s fan-out for one doc to a sustainable rate. (4) Shard the op log by docId so aggregate load is never the bottleneck — only per-doc is.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** one collab server handles ~10K ordered ops/s for a single doc. A doc with 5,000 active typers × 5 ops/s = 25K ops/s → exceeds one server. Fix: cap real-time editors at ~100 (→ ~500 ops/s, comfortable) and batch the other 4,900 as viewers at 4 updates/s → ~20K *broadcast* msgs/s, fanned out by a separate read-replica tier, not the ordering authority.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Two ops same position | Second transformed via OT | Both survive, converge |
| Op log append fails | No ack sent | Client retries same `clientOpId` (idempotent) |
| Collab server crashes mid-edit | In-flight unacked ops lost to client | Client replays unacked ops on reconnect from `baseRevision`; durable log is source of truth |
| Client offline 2 hours | Big divergence | CRDT merge by char-id, or replay+transform if within op-log window |
| Hot doc 5K editors | One authority saturates | Cap real-time editors; batch viewers via replica fan-out |
| Duplicate op (retry) | Same `clientOpId` seen twice | Dedup store rejects the duplicate |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "My default is server-authoritative OT: one server per doc orders edits and transforms any that collided, so two people typing in the same spot both win. It's lean and it's what Docs really used. The two things OT is weak at are long-offline merges and one massively shared doc. For offline I can fall back to a CRDT representation, where every character has a unique id so divergent copies merge with no referee — I pay per-character metadata for that, so I only reach for it when offline is in scope. For the hot doc, I cap how many people edit in real time and turn the rest into viewers who get batched updates a few times a second, which collapses the fan-out to something one replica tier can serve. The bottleneck was always per-document ordering, never aggregate, so sharding by docId handles the rest."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just use CRDTs everywhere?":** "CRDTs are beautiful for offline and decentralization, but they carry per-character metadata (tombstones for deletes, unique ids) that inflates memory and document size, and ordering/interleaving anomalies still need care. For the common case — people editing online through our servers — OT is leaner and lower-latency, so I use CRDTs only where offline merge actually earns the cost."

### How does the stateful collab tier survive a server crash?

#### Good: Sticky routing + durable op log
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Send a doc's editors to one server, but write every edit to durable storage first, so if that server dies a new one rebuilds from storage."
**Approach:** a routing layer maps docId → owning collab server via consistent hashing and a registry. Every op is appended to the durable, replicated op log *before* ack. On crash, the registry reassigns the doc to a healthy server, which loads the latest snapshot + replays ops since.
**⚠️ What breaks:** during the seconds of failover, editors are disconnected; unacked in-flight ops aren't yet durable.
**🔁 Forces upgrade:** wanting near-zero-disruption failover.

#### Great: Warm standby + client replay
**↩️ Previous tier:** cold failover meant a visible stall and risked unacked ops.
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Keep a hot backup ready and have clients re-send anything not yet confirmed, so a crash is barely noticeable."
**Approach:** the op log is synchronously replicated (RPO≈0 for acked ops). Clients hold unacked ops keyed by `clientOpId` and replay them on reconnect; the new server dedups by that key. A warm standby per shard cuts RTO to seconds.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** snapshot every 100 ops → on failover a server replays ≤100 ops ≈ a few ms to be current. **✅** acked op never lost (sync replication); unacked op safely replayed (idempotent by `clientOpId`).

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The collab server is stateful, so failure handling is mostly about making it *recoverable*, not stateless. Every acknowledged op is in a synchronously replicated log before I ack, so it's never lost. A routing registry reassigns a dead doc to a healthy server, which is current within milliseconds because we snapshot every hundred ops. Clients keep their unacked edits and replay them on reconnect, deduped by a client op id, so a crash costs a brief blip, not data."

> 🧠 **If they ask "what about a network partition splitting editors?":** "Only the server that owns the doc in the registry can commit; a partitioned-off server can't ack, so clients on the wrong side reconnect to the owner. We favor consistency over availability for the ordering authority — better a 3-second reconnect than two divergent histories."

### How do we keep storage bounded as op logs grow forever?

#### Good: Snapshot + compact
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Take periodic full snapshots and throw away the fine-grained edits older than that, keeping only what you need to rebuild."
**Approach:** snapshot every N ops; once a snapshot covers ops ≤ R, ops below R can be compacted/tiered. Recent ops (30 days) stay hot for fine undo; older ops tier to cold storage or are dropped past the snapshot.
**⚠️ What breaks:** fine-grained undo beyond the retention window is lost (acceptable per N4 assumption).

#### Great: Tiered retention + cold archive
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Hot recent edits on fast storage, full history as cheap cold archives."
**Approach:** hot op log (recent) on fast store; snapshots in object storage; cold ops archived compressed. **🔢** 18 TB/day raw ops compresses ~5–10×; after compacting to snapshots, retained footprint is a few PB, dominated by snapshots, not ops.
**✅ Failure matrix:** snapshot corrupt → rebuild from prior snapshot + ops; op-log gap → snapshot is the recovery floor.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Op logs would grow without bound, so I snapshot every hundred ops and compact: recent ops stay hot for fine undo, everything older is represented by snapshots plus cold-archived ops. Raw ops are tens of terabytes a day but compress well and mostly get folded into snapshots, so the steady-state footprint is a few petabytes dominated by snapshots — cheap object storage, not the database."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not keep every op forever?":** "We can for cold archive, but for *live* serving it's wasteful — replaying a million ops to open a doc is slow. Snapshots bound replay; the full op archive is there if we ever need forensic-grade history."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean)**

Read/open path target: **99.99% (four nines)** — about **52 minutes/year** of downtime. Edit path target: **99.95%** — about **4.4 hours/year**, slightly lower because the stateful tier has failover stalls. Mechanisms: stateless read services behind multiple instances; **multi-AZ** *(multiple data centres in the same region, so if one building loses power the others take over automatically)* for every store; CDN/edge cache for snapshots; warm standbys for collab shards; the durable op log as the source of truth.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if it goes down | Graceful degradation (what the USER SEES) | Recovery action |
|---|---|---|---|
| API Gateway | No new opens/shares | Existing live sockets keep working; new opens fail briefly | Multi-instance, health-checked; auto-replace |
| Document Service | Can't load new docs | "Couldn't open, retry" — already-open docs unaffected | Stateless; scale/restart |
| Collab Server | Editors of its docs disconnect | "Reconnecting…" for ~seconds; unacked edits replayed, nothing lost | Registry reassigns doc to standby; replay from log |
| Op Log | Can't commit edits | Edits queue client-side, "saving…"; reads still work | Sync-replicated; failover to replica |
| Snapshot Store | Slow/failed opens | Serve from edge cache; cold opens reconstruct from op log | Object storage is 11-nines durable; cache absorbs |
| Metadata DB | Can't resolve revision/pointer | New opens fail; live editing continues | Multi-AZ replica failover |
| Permissions Service | Can't change/verify shares | Cached decisions still enforce; sharing UI degraded | Cache TTL backstop; failover |
| Redis (presence) | Cursors/online list vanish | Cursors disappear; **editing fully unaffected** | Rebuilds from heartbeats in seconds |

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class**

**RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much recent data we can afford to lose if it crashes right now; RPO≈0 means we lose nothing because every write is replicated before we ack. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back after a failure.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Committed ops (your edits) | ≈ 0 | seconds | Synchronous replication before ack; client replay of unacked |
| Doc metadata | ≈ 0 | seconds | Multi-AZ sync replicas |
| Snapshots | minutes (rebuildable from ops) | minutes | Object storage durability + regeneration |
| Permissions | ≈ 0 | seconds | Replicated SQL |
| Presence | N/A (disposable) | seconds | Rebuilt from live heartbeats |

**9D — Cost breakdown**

Top drivers: (1) **Collab server fleet (compute)** — ~6M persistent connections need ~125+ stateful servers always warm, plus standbys; persistent CPU/memory is the dominant line, not storage. (2) **Op-log write + storage throughput** — millions of durable, replicated writes/sec is write-amplified by replication. (3) **Snapshot storage + egress** — a few PB at rest is cheap, but serving snapshots to opens drives CDN/egress cost. Rough order-of-magnitude: low-to-mid **hundreds of thousands of dollars/month**, dominated by always-on collab compute. **Biggest single optimization:** batch and compress op broadcasts (and cap real-time editors on hot docs) — it cuts both fan-out compute and bandwidth, the two largest costs.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When the collab server for your doc dies, you see 'Reconnecting' for a couple seconds, then your unacked keystrokes replay and you're back — nothing lost, because every acked edit was already in a replicated log. If Redis dies, cursors blink out but your editing never pauses. If snapshot storage hiccups, hot docs still open from cache and cold ones rebuild from the op log. The expensive part isn't storage — it's keeping millions of live connections warm, so the biggest cost lever is batching the broadcasts."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Server-authoritative OT vs CRDT-everywhere**
1. **What we chose & why:** OT for the online path — lean memory, low latency, proven for text.
2. **What we gave up:** clean offline/long-divergence merges and freedom from a per-doc authority.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Like having one referee call the order of plays — fast and unambiguous when everyone's on the field, but useless if a player's been off the field for hours."
4. **When this reverses:** if offline editing (hours disconnected) becomes a core requirement, or we want truly decentralized/multi-master editing, I flip the core to CRDTs and pay the per-character metadata cost.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I lead with OT because for online editing it's leaner and lower-latency; the day offline-for-hours is in scope, CRDTs earn their overhead and I switch."

**🔀 Decision: Single home region / single authority per doc vs multi-master global**
1. **What we chose & why:** one owning server (and home region) per doc gives a single clean ordering point.
2. **What we gave up:** cross-region editors pay the home region's latency; the authority is a per-doc single point of serialization.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Everyone routes to one cash register per store — no double-counting, but a customer across town waits in that store's line."
4. **When this reverses:** if a huge fraction of docs are truly global with editors split across continents, I'd invest in regional CRDT replicas with async convergence.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "One authority per doc keeps convergence simple and correct; I'd only go multi-master if cross-region co-editing became the norm, because it makes the conflict story much harder."

**🔀 Decision: Append-only op log + periodic snapshots vs store-latest-only**
1. **What we chose & why:** keep every op (for a window) + snapshots — enables convergence, durability, and history.
2. **What we gave up:** storage volume (~18 TB/day raw) and a compaction pipeline to maintain.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Keeping the full logbook plus periodic photos, instead of just the latest photo — more to store, but you can rewind to any moment."
4. **When this reverses:** if history and fine undo were dropped from scope, store-latest-plus-short-buffer would be far cheaper.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I keep the op log because it's what makes both convergence and version history possible; the price is storage, which I control with snapshots and compaction."

**🔀 Decision: Strong consistency (CP) for the ordering authority vs always-available (AP)**
1. **What we chose & why:** during a partition, only the registered owner commits — we pick consistency over availability for ordering.
2. **What we gave up:** a few seconds of edit availability on the wrong side of a partition (clients reconnect).
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "If the phone line splits, only one side gets to be the official scorekeeper — the other side waits rather than keep a second, conflicting scorebook."
4. **When this reverses:** never for the ordering authority — divergent histories are unacceptable; presence/cursors already lean AP since they're disposable.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "For ordering I'm CP on purpose — a three-second reconnect beats two documents that silently disagree. Presence is the opposite, AP, because a stale cursor is harmless."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The three decisions I'd flag: I chose OT over CRDTs for leanness, and I'd flip to CRDTs the day long-offline editing is core. I chose one authority per doc for clean convergence, trading cross-region latency, and I'd only go multi-master if co-editing went truly global. And I keep a full op log for history and convergence, paying storage I manage with snapshots. Underneath all of them is one principle — protect per-document convergence above everything — which is also why I'm consistency-over-availability for the ordering path."
