## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two people type at the exact same character position in the same instant — who wins and is anything lost?"**
**The mechanism:** both ops arrive at the one collab server with the same `baseRevision`. The first commits at R+1; the second is transformed (OT) against the first — e.g. its insert index shifts past the first insert — and commits at R+2. Both survive; everyone applies them in the same committed order and converges.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Neither loses. A referee orders them and nudges the second edit's position so both fit, and everyone ends up with the same text."
**💬 One-liner:** "Nobody loses — the server orders them and transforms the second so both inserts land and everyone converges."

**❓ "What happens when a collab server crashes mid-edit?"**
**The mechanism:** acked ops are already in a synchronously replicated log, so they're safe. The routing registry reassigns the doc to a warm standby that loads the latest snapshot + replays the few ops since. Clients replay their unacked ops by `clientOpId`, deduped on the server.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Your saved edits are safe in replicated storage; you see 'reconnecting' for a moment, your last few keystrokes re-send automatically, and you're back."
**💬 One-liner:** "Acked edits are durable; clients replay the unacked ones, so a crash is a blip, not data loss."

**❓ "A public doc suddenly has 5,000 people editing — how do you not melt?"**
**The mechanism:** one authority can order ~10K ops/s for a doc; 5,000 typers would blow past that. We cap *real-time editors* (~100) and demote the rest to viewers receiving *batched* broadcasts (~4/s) fanned out by a read-replica tier, not the ordering authority.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Only a hundred or so edit live; everyone else watches with updates a few times a second, which keeps one document from overwhelming one server."
**💬 One-liner:** "Cap live editors, batch everyone else as viewers off a replica tier — the hot doc degrades, it doesn't fall over."

**❓ "Can the same edit be applied twice (e.g. a retry after a network blip)?"**
**The mechanism:** every op carries a `clientOpId`; the server keeps a dedup set per doc and rejects a duplicate, returning the original ack. So a retried submit is idempotent.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Each edit has a unique ticket; if the same ticket shows up twice, the server ignores the duplicate."
**💬 One-liner:** "Every op has an idempotency key, so retries are deduped — never applied twice."

**❓ "Can editors' copies permanently diverge?"**
**The mechanism:** no — a single authority assigns a total order per doc, and OT transforms guarantee that applying ops in that order yields identical state everywhere; clients reconcile via the revision in each ack/broadcast.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "There's one official order of edits, so everyone replays the same edits the same way and can't drift apart."
**💬 One-liner:** "One total order per doc plus correct transforms means convergence is guaranteed, not hoped for."

**❓ "How do you stop a view-only user from injecting edits via the browser console?"**
**The mechanism:** the WebSocket token is scoped to the doc *and* role; the collab server checks the cached ACL on *every* op and rejects edits from a viewer. Identity comes from the signed token, never a client-supplied field.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "The server, not the browser, decides what you're allowed to do — a viewer's edit ops are simply refused."
**💬 One-liner:** "Every op is authorized server-side against the ACL — a viewer's forged edits get rejected, full stop."

**❓ "How do you revoke someone mid-session?"**
**The mechanism:** a permission change pushes an invalidation to the collab server, which drops the cached ACL entry and disconnects that user's live session, rejecting further ops; the short cache TTL is the backstop if the push is missed.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "The moment you remove someone, we kick their live session within seconds, not whenever they next open the doc."
**💬 One-liner:** "Revocation pushes a kick to the live tier — out within seconds, with a TTL backstop."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"**
**The mechanism:** always-on collab compute for ~6M persistent connections dominates, ahead of storage. Batching/compressing op broadcasts and capping real-time editors on hot docs cuts both fan-out CPU and egress — the two biggest levers.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Keeping millions of live connections open is the pricey part, not storing data — so we shrink the chatter on those connections."
**💬 One-liner:** "It's live-connection compute, not storage; batching broadcasts is the biggest cost lever."

**❓ "How would you add comments / suggestion mode later?"**
**The mechanism:** comments are anchored to a *range* (start/end char ids) and stored separately from content ops; they ride the same op pipeline as a distinct op type, so they transform/anchor as text shifts. Suggestion mode is edits stored as pending ops not yet merged into the canonical revision.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Comments pin to a span of text and move with it; suggestions are edits parked to the side until someone accepts them."
**💬 One-liner:** "Comments are range-anchored side data on the same op pipeline; suggestions are pending ops merged on accept."

**❓ "How would you support offline editing for hours?"**
**The mechanism:** switch the doc model to a CRDT so a long-divergent client merges by character id without a transform backlog; on reconnect we merge both histories deterministically. This is the case where CRDT overhead is worth paying.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We'd give every character a permanent id so a laptop edited offline all day can merge cleanly when it reconnects."
**💬 One-liner:** "Offline-for-hours is the CRDT case — character ids let divergent copies merge with no referee."

**❓ "How do you keep doc loads fast for a five-year-old document?"**
**The mechanism:** loads read the latest *snapshot* + revision, not the full op log; the socket streams only ops since that revision. Snapshots are cached at the edge. So load cost is independent of document age.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We open from a recent save-point and only fill in what changed since, so an old doc opens as fast as a new one."
**💬 One-liner:** "Load = snapshot + revision; age doesn't matter because we never replay full history."

**❓ "How do you guarantee a committed edit is never lost (durability)?"**
**The mechanism:** an op is appended to a synchronously replicated, append-only log *before* the server acks; if the log write fails, no ack is sent and the client retries idempotently. RPO≈0 for acked ops.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We only tell you 'saved' after the edit is safely written to multiple machines — so a crash a second later can't lose it."
**💬 One-liner:** "We ack only after a replicated durable write — acked means truly safe."

**❓ "Why WebSocket instead of HTTP polling or long-poll?"**
**The mechanism:** edits must be *pushed* both ways with sub-200ms latency at high frequency; a persistent WebSocket avoids per-request overhead and lets the server push instantly, where polling adds latency and load.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We keep an open phone line both ways so edits arrive the instant they happen, instead of the client repeatedly asking 'anything new?'"
**💬 One-liner:** "WebSockets give instant two-way push — polling would add latency and hammer the server."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "Google Docs is a system where lots of people edit the same document at once and everyone must end up seeing identical text. The design splits into two halves. The read half is straightforward and stateless: opening a doc fetches a recent snapshot from object storage plus a revision number, fronted by a cache, so even an old document opens instantly. The write half is the hard one: all editors of a doc route to a single collab server that's the authority for ordering, and when two edits collide it uses operational transformation to rewrite the second so both survive — no lost updates, guaranteed convergence — appending each op to a synchronously replicated log before acking so nothing acknowledged is ever lost. The two halves connect through an async snapshot worker that periodically materializes content to storage and an op log that feeds both history and recovery. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: protect per-document convergence above all else — which is why I order edits through a single authority, why I'm consistency-over-availability on that path, and why presence and cursors are the one place I happily go fast-and-lossy instead."
